questions.question: Select all that are true about the seeded fake environment Gbench builds before an agentic task runs.
choices: It creates fake files, fake callable tools, and a known starting backend state.
It uses a production database of real user records as the starting state.
It ranks already-released models on a public leaderboard.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Seeding builds fake files, fake tools, and a deterministic starting backend state so the harness knows the world at time zero. It is not a real production database or a public ranking.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the parts that make up an eval harness.
choices: An environment the model acts inside.
A trigger that starts the model running as an agent.
A grader that decides the pass or fail result.
None of the above
questions.explanation: An eval harness is environment plus trigger plus grader, so all three listed parts are real. That leaves None of the above unselected.

questions.question: Select all that are true about where the Ditto eval runner runs and what it grades.
choices: Ditto runs as a job on Borg, Google's cluster job scheduler.
The model Ditto grades is hosted on GCP and exposes an API endpoint.
Ditto runs inside the GCP hosted model endpoint itself.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Ditto is a Borg job and the model it grades lives on a separate GCP hosted endpoint. Ditto does not run inside that endpoint, so the third claim is false.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the Borg to GCP crossover.
choices: It is the bridge a request travels from a Borg job to a GCP hosted endpoint and back.
It must handle authentication between the two systems.
It must handle network reachability and request routing between the two worlds.
It requires retraining the model from scratch on every eval run.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The crossover bridges a Borg job to a GCP endpoint and covers auth, networking, and routing. It has nothing to do with retraining the model, so that claim is false.

questions.question: Select all that are true about what a working Borg to GCP crossover lets the eval skip entirely.
choices: Authenticating a Borg job to the GCP endpoint.
Routing the request across the two systems.
Letting responses travel back to the eval job.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A working crossover skips none of these. It still performs auth, routing, and the return path, it just performs them reliably, so None of the above is the only correct choice.

questions.question: Select all that are true about what a Terraform plan gives you before any resource changes.
choices: A readable diff of exactly what will be created, changed, or destroyed.
A finished training run against the new model build.
A quantized artifact ready for release.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Plan shows a reviewable diff of what will change before apply performs it. It is not a training run or a quantized artifact.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about the one shot reproducible run lifecycle.
choices: A Cloud Run one shot job keeps running as a long lived server after it finishes.
The fifteen page manual document is what makes each run deterministic.
A Docker image pins the exact code and a specific benchmark commit so the inputs are deterministic.
Terraform provisions the cloud resources from a declarative config.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The false statements are the NOT-true selections. A one shot job exits rather than staying up, and the manual doc is the unreproducible thing being replaced. Docker pinning and Terraform provisioning are both true.

questions.question: Select all that are true about idempotent teardown in a one shot eval run.
choices: It is safe to run twice, and running it again changes nothing.
Applying an empty desired set leaves no resources behind, so runs do not keep billing.
It leaves resources running after the job to save setup time on the next run.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Idempotent teardown is safe to repeat and returns to an empty state so nothing lingers or bills. Leaving resources running is the opposite of teardown, so that claim is false.
